title: Reliability (Workshop)
text:

How tired are you now?

title: Reliability (Workshop)
text:
I created this new thermometer and measured the temperature in my office:
Is it reliable?

title: Reliability (Workshop)
text:
Work in groups
Use post-its (one per idea)
Answer the following questions:

what is reliability?
how can I determine if my thermometer is reliable?

Propose as many solutions as you can.



~20 minutes group work
~12 minutes to present ideas
~8 minutes discussion

title: Reliability (Workshop)
text:
Homework

What was it like to answer that same question 3 times? Was it 3 times the same?
For next time read the 1 page section Reliability in Wu et al. (2016) “Chapter 1: What is measurement?”
Is that definition useful? Does it make sense?

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Different types of measurement?

Are measurements in physics and in psychology different?
If so, how?

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
The position that measurements in psychology and physics are qualitatively different is very common:

“The difficulty with measurement in the psycho-social world is that the attributes of interest are generally not directly visible to us as objects of the physical world are. It is only through observable indicator variables of the attributes that measurements can be made. For example, currently there is no machine that can directly measure depression.” (p1)



M. Wu et al. (2016),  Chapter 1: What is Measurement? Educational Measurement for Applied Researchers, DOI 10.1007/978-981-10-3302-5_1

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Hand, David J.,
Measurement: A Very Short Introduction, Very Short Introductions (Oxford, 2016; online edn, Oxford Academic, 27 Oct. 2016), https://doi.org/10.1093/actrade/9780198779568.001.0001.

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Representational

length
weight
…


Pragmatic

cost of living
Apgar score
…



the two extremes of a continuum

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Representational measurement

We assign numbers (using a specific procedure) to different objects, so that the relationships between numbers correspond to the relationships between the objects.
They reflect a property of the world, which exists and is defined independently of the specific measurement procedure.

title: Different types of Measurement
text: Does this make sense?

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Pragmatic measurement

We design / construct a measure (with desirable properties) for a specific purpose.
Pragmatic measurement “simultaneously defines the attribute being measured and specifies how to measure it”.

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Exercise:

Create a measure of the “cost of living in Luxembourg”.

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
each suggestion will likely be slightly different
none is wrong or correct
what exactly it measures is defined by how exactly that measure is constructed

title: Different types of Measurement
Different types of Measurement
text:

Blind men and an elphant

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
The methodological question:
How exactly should we “aggregate” numbers into scores?

summing?
averaging?
model fit?
weighting or not?
optimize for specific purpose?

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Exercise:
Find examples in psychology of different ways of aggregating numbers into a score.

Questionnaires (e.g., GAD-7)

Educational assessments (e.g., grades)

text:

The Thermoscope
The Thermometer

Controlling confounding variables
The triangulation of methods
Theory development:

conceptual clarity
consistency
predictive power

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Stevens’ taxonomy of measurement levels
Measurement levels: (N-O-I-R)

what are admissible transformations?
what are equivalent instruments?
what types of data analysis are legit?

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Permissible transformations & measurement levels:

Can we create a language that is “equivalent” to Luxembourgish?
Is a °C thermometer equivalent to a °F thermometer?
is a °C thermometer equivalent to a thermoscope?

title: Different types of Measurement
text: Permissible transformations & Measurement scales

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Levels of Measurement
wikipedia definition:

“Level of measurement or scale of measure is a classification that describes the nature of information within the values assigned to variables.”

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Examples:

Nominal metal becomes red: “it’s hot”: identify a particular state
Ordinal thermoscope: shows ordered variations
Interval  thermometer (°C): intervals are meaningful
Ratio thermometer (K): there is an absolute zero

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Key insights #1
By throwing away information, I can move down the ladder (and vice versa)

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Key insights #2
Each measurement level includes a set of arbitrary choices
Ratio has fewer arbitrary choices than Interval.

title: Different types of Measurement
Different types of Measurement
text:
Key insight #3
The level of measurement dictates how that data can be analyzed (i.e., valid math operations), and what type of statements can be made based on those measurements.

title: Different types of Measurement
text: Is 20°C twice as hot as 10°C?

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
It is obviously true that
\[20 = 2 \times 10\]
However, for interval-level measurements (assignment of numbers to objects), multiplication is NOT allowed.

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
\[10°C = 50°F\]


\[20°C = 68°F \neq 100°F\]



cash recipients in “lower-income countries gained three times more happiness than those in higher-income countries”

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Is an IQ of 120 twice an IQ of 60?
Is a 120cm tall child twice as tall as a 60cm child?


“The answer is not included in the numbers”.

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Key insight #4
Because measurement levels imply some level of arbitrariness (e.g., choice of unit), all else being equal, measures at the same level of measurement are informationally equivalent.
 Example: I can convert °C to °F and vice versa without losing information (the numbers are different, but the information is the same).

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Key insight #5
The level of measurement is a property of the measurement, not of the construct:


Temperature is theorized as a continuous positive quantity.


Depending on the instrument used,

we will have measures that are of type Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio
we will be allowed to do certain operations, but not others
we will be allowed to make certain types of quantitative statements

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
Caution:
It’s easy to make the mistake of assigning numeric properties to reality when using numbers to represent measurements!

title: Different types of Measurement
Different types of Measurement
text: Does this actually matter?

text: A.P. Pedersen, D. Kellen, C. Mayo-Wilson, C.P. Davis-Stober, J.C. Dunn, M.A. Khan, M.B. Stinchcombe, M.L. Kalish, K. Tentori, & J. Haaf, Discourse on measurement, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 122 (5) e2401229121, https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2401229121 (2025).

title: Different types of Measurement
text:
exposure to “lead is responsible for the loss of 824,097,690 IQ points as of 2015” (1),
cash recipients in “lower-income countries gained three times more happiness than those in higher-income countries” (2),
humans choose “numerical answers in a systematic way as though they sense within themselves–and can communicate–a reliable numerical scale for their feelings” (3).



M. J. McFarland, M. E. Hauer, A. Reuben, Half of us population exposed to adverse lead levels in early childhood. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 119, e2118631119 (2022).
R. J. Dwyer, E. W. Dunn, Wealth redistribution promotes happiness. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 119, e2211123119 (2022).
C. Kaiser, A. J. Oswald, The scientific value of numerical measures of human feelings. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 119, e2210412119 (2022).

title: Measuring the Mind
text:
Based on the “What is measurement?” paper (see Documents in Moodle), or any other document, answer the following questions:

What are some classic definitions of measurement?
What do you think of them?
What are the main keywords/concepts typically covered in measurement theory accounts?



Submit your responses in Moodle: assignment: “key concepts and definitions” as 1 PDF file following the convention <last_name>_definitions.pdf


You can/should work in groups, but everyone must submit a document (can be the same for all group members).


Don’t use LLMs blindly; think for yourself!

title: Measuring the Mind
text:
Use cases
Sometimes theory prevents us from seeing reality.

We will see a few examples where measurement plays/played a central role (positively or negatively).
The goal is to evaluate to what extent the classic psychological measurement theory is adequate and useful in those various scenarios.

title: Measuring the Mind
text:
This is the (work in progress) course website for “Measuring the Mind: Advanced Psychological Measurement Theory”.
In this course we will cover the following content:

Chapter 1: Introduction
Chapter 2: History of measurements
Chapter 3: Different types of measurements
Chapter 4: Reliability

Each chapter may contain links to slides and possibly other resources.


This course aims to provides a critical and applied treatment of psychological measurement theory. We will cover a broad range of topics, ranging from the philosophical (what does it mean to measure something?) to the applied (how to conduct Item Response Theory analysis in R?).



Navigate through the course materials using the sidebar on the left. Each chapter includes:

Lecture notes
Slides
Practical exercises in R
Additional resources

title: Course Overview
text: Advanced Psychological Measurement Theory covers the theoretical foundations and practical applications of modern psychometric methods. The course explores both classical and contemporary approaches to psychological assessment and test development. It will present current challenges in psychological measurement and encourage critical thinking.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
Measurement is a fundamental building block of science: without measurement we can’t build, refine or disprove theories, without theories we would not know what to measure. Across the history of science theory and measurements co-evolve, each benefitting the other.
As scientists, but more generally, as a citizen aiming to understand the world around us, it is important to reflect on what it means to measure something and how to do so. This course will cover measurement from a diversity of perspectives. The goal is not to “sell” a specific theory of measurement in psychology but instead to raise awareness about the importance of measurement theory in psychology and to develop your critical thinking in this domain. At the end of the course you may not have more answeres, but you certainly will have more questions.

text: In this chapter we will review different ways in which measurements have been classified in types and ask the question of how measurements in psychology are similar or different from measurements in physics.

title: Chapter 5: Latent Variable Theory
text: In this chapter we move beyond Classical Test Theory and introduce latent variable theory — the idea that observed responses are caused by unobservable latent variables reflecting psychological attributes. As an example model in this category, we will explore Item Response Theory (IRT), a prominent model in the world of educational assessment and which models the probability of a given item response as a function of a person’s latent trait and the statistical properties of each item.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Recap
We listed several approaches to determine the reliability of a thermometer.
The same approaches did not seem to work well for questionnaire data.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Classical Test Theory (CTT)
In psychometrics, “reliability” is most often associated to “Classic Test Theory” (aka “True Score Theory”).

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
“[Lord and Novick (1968)’s] treatment of the classical test model […] is arguably the most influential treatise on psychological measurement in the history of psychology.”



[…] few psychologists know about the other approaches […] but every psychologist knows about true scores, random error, and reliability–the core concepts of classical test theory” (p11)



Borsboom D. Measuring the Mind: Conceptual Issues in Contemporary Psychometrics. Cambridge University Press; 2005.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
What people say about it.
CTT is still relevant today:


“The fact that CTT was developed before IRT does not mean that CTT is outdated or replaced by IRT. Both CTT and IRT provide useful statistics to help us analyze data. […] For many item analyses, CTT may be sufficient to provide the information we need.”




“[…] we stress that CTT is an important part of the methodologies for educational and psychological measurement. In particular, the exposition of the concept of reliability in CTT sets the basis for evaluating measuring instruments.”



Wu et al. (2016) Educational Measurement for Applied Researchers, Chapter 5: Classic Test Theory

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
What people say about it.
CTT is fundamentally flawed:


“In the context of psychological measurement, the stated assumptions are unrealistic […]” (p16)




“Basically, the classical test theorist is trying to sell you shoes which are obviously three sizes too small.” (p20)




“[…] 95 per cent of researchers involved in research in differential psychology are probably not doing what they think they are doing. For no concept in test theory has been so prone to misinterpretation as the true score.” (p31)




“[…] the platonic true score interpretation is like an alien in a B-movie: no matter how hard you beat it up, it keeps coming back.” (p32)



Borsboom D. Measuring the Mind: Conceptual Issues in Contemporary Psychometrics. Cambridge University Press; 2005.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Measuring the Mind






Borsboom D. Measuring the Mind: Conceptual Issues in Contemporary Psychometrics. Cambridge University Press; 2005.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
What do you think?

What is CTT?
What are its main criticisms?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Goal
Our main goal is to determine the reliability of our questionnaire.
To do so we will go through some “theoretical acrobatics” (p31)

Borsboom D. Measuring the Mind: Conceptual Issues in Contemporary Psychometrics. Cambridge University Press; 2005.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
core idea #1

CTT defines the true score of person \(i\), \(t_i\) as the expectation of the observed score \(X_i\) over replications:
\[ t_i \equiv \mathbb{E}[X_i]\]


We can never observe \(t_i\)!

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
core idea #2

CTT defines the error score \(E_i\) as the difference between the observed score the true score:
\[E_i \equiv X_i - t_i \]



Note: \(X_i\) and \(E_i\) are random variables, \(t_i\) is by definition a constant.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
core idea #3 (main equation)

When testing multiple people, an extra source of randomness is introduced by sampling from a population.
\[X = T + E\]

(i.e., true score is now also a random variable)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
In words…
Each person \(i\) has an observed score \(x_i\). CTT states that this \(x_i\) can be broken down into \(t_i\) which is that person’s true score, and \(e_i\) the measurement error which explains why the observed score does not perfectly match the true score.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
That same person could have responded somewhat differently on that test: in that case the value of \(e_i\) would be different and the value of \(x_i\) would also be different, but the value of \(t_i\) would be the same.


(i.e., \(X_i\) and \(E_i\) are viewed as random variables and \(x_i\) and \(e_i\) are specific realisations of those RAs).

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
By CTT definition, if we were to compute the average across replications of the test score, we would get an estimate of the person’s true score.
 Defining \(t_i \equiv \mathbb{E}[X_i]\) necessarily implies \(\mathbb{E}[E_i]=0\)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text: By CTT definition, the true scores and the error scores are independent from each other (i.e., no correlation)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Do these assumptions make sense?

the true score is unknown
what does “could have” mean?
setting true score to be equal the expectation of the test score is problematic (e.g., assumes no bias)
true score definition is inherently tied to a specific test

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Reliability
Given \(X = T + E\),
how would you define reliability?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Reliability
… the fraction of the observed variance \(\sigma^2_X\) that is due to variation in true scores \(\sigma^2_T\) as opposed to random error \(\sigma^2_E\)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Given that \(T\) and \(E\) are independent (by definition),
\[\sigma^2_X = \sigma^2_T + \sigma^2_E\]

We can define reliability as
\[
\rho^2_{XT} = \frac{\sigma^2_T}{\sigma^2_X} = \frac{\sigma^2_T}{\sigma^2_T + \sigma^2_E}
\]

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Computer simulations
set.seed(42)

# true score parameters
m <- 100 #  mean of true score in the population
s <- 15  # standard deviation of true score in the population

# error score parameters
s_err <- 20

# simulating data
n <- 500
true_scores <- rnorm(n, m, s)
error_scores <- rnorm(n, 0, s_err)
observed_scores <- true_scores + error_scores

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Exercise
Calculate the reliability
(measuring-the-mind-labs on github)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
section: Reliability depends on the population tested!
text:
Reliability depends on the population tested!
Intuition:


Low reliability \(\rho \approx 0\)
Most variance is noise — the instrument is unstable

High reliability \(\rho \approx 1\)
Most variance reflects real differences between people



Reliability tells us how much we can trust that differences in scores reflect real differences in the underlying attribute.


It is not the variance of the error!

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Are we done?
Can we do this in practice?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
In practice, we obviously don’t know the true scores, so we can’t compute \(\sigma^2_T\) and thus we can’t use that equation for computing reliability.

How do we get out of this impasse?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Parallel tests!
Assume that people complete the same test twice (with “brainwashing”).
\[x_i^{(1)} = t_i + e_i^{(1)}\]
\[x_i^{(2)} = t_i + e_i^{(2)}\]
we assume also that \(cov(E^{(1)}, E^{(2)}) = 0\)
Intuitively, the correlation between \(X^{(1)}\) and \(X^{(2)}\) tells us something about reliability.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Reliability defined as correlation between parallel tests

changing notation for consistency: \[\rho_{XX'} = \rho_{X^{(1)}X^{(2)}}\]


applying the correlation formula: \[
\rho_{XX'} = \frac{cov(XX')}{\sigma_{X} \sigma_{X'}}
\]


based on CTT assumptions:
\[
\rho_{XX'} = \frac{cov(XX')}{\sigma_{X} \sigma_{X'}} = \frac{\sigma^2_T}{\sigma^2_X}
\]

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Recall our little experiment:

How tired are you now? On a scale from 1 (fully awake) to 10 (almost asleep)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text: Can we consider these 3 completions of the test as being “equivalent”?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text: What does “error” mean in this context?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Exercise
Compute the reliability on this dataset (see notebook)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Four main approaches

Test-retest reliability
Parallel forms reliability
Internal consistency
Inter-rater reliability

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
1. Test-retest reliability
Administer the same instrument twice to the same people, separated by a time interval.
\[r_{tt} = \text{cor}(X_{\text{time 1}},\ X_{\text{time 2}})\]

Problem: how long to wait?

Too short → carry-over effects (memory)
Too long → true change in the attribute

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
2. Parallel forms reliability
Create two equivalent versions of the instrument and administer both to the same people.

Parallel forms must satisfy:

Equal true score means: \(\mathbb{E}[X_1] = \mathbb{E}[X_2]\)
Equal error variances: \(\sigma^2_{E_1} = \sigma^2_{E_2}\)



Problem: constructing truly parallel forms is difficult in practice.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
3. Internal consistency
Estimate reliability from a single administration using the covariance structure among items.

The key idea: if items measure the same construct, they should correlate with each other.

chapters/chapter4/slides/reliability.qmd ## Split-half reliability {.smaller .center}
Split the test into two halves (e.g., odd vs. even items) and correlate their scores.
\[r_{\text{split-half}} = \text{cor}(X_{\text{half 1}},\ X_{\text{half 2}})\]

Apply the Spearman-Brown correction to estimate full-test reliability:
\[r_{SB} = \frac{2\, r_{\text{split-half}}}{1 + r_{\text{split-half}}}\]


Problem: the result depends on how you split the test.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Cronbach’s \(\alpha\)
Cronbach’s \(\alpha\) generalises split-half reliability by averaging over all possible split-halves:
\[\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)\]


\(k\) = number of items
\(\sigma^2_{Y_i}\) = variance of item \(i\)
\(\sigma^2_X\) = variance of the total score



\(\alpha\) is a lower bound on reliability — it equals reliability only when the \(\tau\)-equivalence assumption holds.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Interpreting \(\alpha\)
Common (rough) guidelines:



\(\alpha\)
Interpretation




\(\geq .90\)
Excellent


\(.80 - .89\)
Good


\(.70 - .79\)
Acceptable


\(.60 - .69\)
Questionable


\(< .60\)
Poor





These thresholds are just conventions.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
4. Inter-rater reliability
When scores are assigned by human judges, we want to know how consistently different raters agree.

Common indices:

Percent agreement (simple but ignores chance)
Cohen’s \(\kappa\) (corrects for chance agreement)
Intraclass correlation (ICC) (for continuous ratings)

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
The Standard Error of Measurement (SEM) expresses reliability in the original unit of the scale:
\[SEM = \sigma_X \sqrt{1 - \rho_{XX'}}\]


\(\sigma_X\) = standard deviation of observed scores
\(\rho_{XX'}\) = reliability coefficient

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
The SEM defines a confidence interval around an observed score:
\[T \in [X \pm z \cdot SEM]\]

Example: if a test has \(\sigma_X = 15\), \(\rho = .91\)
\[SEM = 15\sqrt{1 - .91} = 15 \times 0.3 = 4.5\]
A score of 100 → true score likely in [91, 109] (95% CI)



The SEM helps communicate measurement uncertainty in practical, interpretable terms.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
The notion of validity is ill-defined in CTT:
By definition the observed score reflects the true score (whatever that is), therefore the test is necessarily valid.
It’s not clear however what the true score actually is.

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Practical limitations: 
The true score is (too) tightly coupled to the specific test used.

what if someone skips a question?
what if we have limited time to ask questions and want to ask only a subset of questions?
what if we have many questions and we want to ask the questions that are most informative give prior responses (adaptive testing)?

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
“[…] frequently, we place one or two easy items at the beginning of a test to ease any anxiety on the part of the students.”

Is this approach consistent with Classic Test Theory?

Wu et al. Chapter 5: Classic Test Theory p87

title: Reliability and Classic Test Theory (CTT)
text:
Do you think the reliabilty index as defined by CTT is satisfactory?
If your patient fills out a questionnaire and gets a score of 19, what wouly you like a reliability index to tell you about this number?

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Recap
CTT decomposes observed scores into a true score and error:
\[X = T + E\]

The true score is defined as the expectation of the observed score — it is intrinsically tied to a specific test.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Limitations of CTT

The true score has no independent meaning outside the test
What if someone skips a question?
What if we want to give different people different sets of items?
What if we want to adapt which items are asked based on prior responses?


These are not mere inconveniences — they reveal a deeper problem about what CTT is actually measuring.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
CTT does not adequately represent attributes!

Its operationalist view is too restrictive.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Measuring the Mind






Borsboom D. Measuring the Mind: Conceptual Issues in Contemporary Psychometrics. Cambridge University Press; 2005.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Latent variable theory represents the attribute of interest explicitly in the model formulation.
It offers a formal structure that

links test scores to hypothesized attributes (i.e., latent variables)
deduces empirical implications of the model (i.e., conditional independences)
offers a means to test if a model adequately fits empirical data!


Recall that CTT is unfalsifiable by data.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Latent variable models the data-generating mechanism, in which the attribute is represented explicitly as a latent variable.
It requires making choices to constrain the model; these constraints needs to be justified using theory!
Models are underdetermined / the data does not speak for itself.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Types of models in this category

factor analysis (Spearman, 1904)
confirmatory factor analysis
item response theory
latent structure analysis (e.g., latent class analysis)
…

title: Latent Variable Theory
section: A unified framework: “Generalized linear item response theory” (GLIRT)
text:
A unified framework: “Generalized linear item response theory” (GLIRT)
Mellenbergh, G. J. (1994). Generalized linear item response theory. Psychological Bulletin, 115(2), 300–307. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-2909.115.2.300

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
https://benwhalley.github.io/just-enough-r/cfa.html

<! –

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Open questions
What do the arrows mean? - causality?
What is the meaning of the latent variable?
What ontological status do latent variables have? - real vs useful fiction?
–>

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
3 key questions

syntax: how do we relate the observed responses to latent variables?
semantics: how do we interpret the “expected response” to an item?
ontology: what type of things are the latent variables and relationships in the model?


Borsboom D. Measuring the Mind: Conceptual Issues in Contemporary Psychometrics. Cambridge University Press; 2005.

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Syntax: a general form
\[g[\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})] = \beta_j +  \alpha_j * \theta_i\]

\(g()\) := link function
\(\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})\) := expected response of person \(i\) to item \(j\)
\(\theta_i\) := parameter(s) about the person \(i\)
\(\beta_j\), \(\alpha_j\):= parameters about the item \(j\)


There are many variants of this!

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
many variants…
for combinations of

type of observed variable (e.g., continuous, categorical)
type of latent variables (e.g., continuous, categorical)
the link function used (e.g., identity, logit)


Example:

cont, cont, identity: factor analysis
dichotomous, continous, logit: item response theory

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
IRT is a special case of latent variable models;
there are many variants within IRT.

Rasch model (1 parameter model)
Birnbaum model (2 parameter model)
3, 4 parameter models
and many more

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
IRT: core ideas
\[ln[\frac{\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})}{1-\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})}] = \beta_j +  \alpha_j * \theta_i\]

Person and item parameters are combined linearly to produce an output variable
That output variable is the logit of the expected response:


logit is the natural log of the odds ratio;

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Note: It is more common to see IRT equations presented using the inverse link function instead of the link function
link function (logit): \[ln[\frac{\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})}{1-\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})}] = \beta_j +  \alpha_j * \theta_i\]

inverse link function (logistic): \[\mathbb{E}(U_{ij}) = \frac{1}{1+ e^{-(\beta_j +  \alpha_j * \theta_i)}}\]

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Logistic function
\[P(X = 1 \mid \theta) = \frac{e^\theta}{1 + e^\theta} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta}}\]

title: Latent Variable Theory
text:
Exercise
How is this equation different from the one in Classic Test Theory?
\[ln[\frac{\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})}{1-\mathbb{E}(U_{ij})}] = \beta_j +  \alpha_j * \theta_i\]
Recall, CTT:
\[X = T + E \]
